g.data-narration: Welcome to Client-Server Programming. This topic covers socket programming fundamentals essential for network applications.
The client-server model has a client that requests services and a server that listens and responds to those requests.
Sockets are communication endpoints. TCP provides reliable ordered delivery, while UDP offers faster but unreliable communication.
Java's java.net package provides Socket, ServerSocket, DatagramSocket, and InetAddress classes for network programming.
A TCP server creates ServerSocket on a port, calls accept() to wait for clients, then exchanges data using streams.
A TCP client creates a Socket to connect to the server, then uses input/output streams to exchange data.
InetAddress represents IP addresses with methods to get hostnames, IP addresses, and resolve domain names.
A multi-threaded server creates a new thread for each client connection, enabling simultaneous communication with multiple clients.
UDP uses DatagramSocket and DatagramPacket for connectionless communication. Each send/receive is independent with no streams.
g.text: len, address, port) // no connect needed

g.data-narration: The Echo Server example shows a complete TCP server that receives a message and sends it back with 'Echo:' prefix.
g.text: Socket s = ss.accept(); // Wait for client

g.data-narration: Step-by-step: Server creates ServerSocket on port 5000, waits for accept(), gets streams, reads message, echoes it back, then closes.
The Echo Client connects to the server, sends a message, receives the echoed response, and displays it.
The Chat Server uses Vector to store all client PrintWriters and broadcasts messages to all connected clients.
Port numbers range from 0-65535. Well-known ports 0-1023 need admin access, registered ports 1024-49151 for applications.
Common mistakes: forgetting to close resources, using wrong ports, not handling IOException, confusing TCP and UDP, and blocking operations.
Key exam points: Remember port numbers, TCP vs UDP differences, stream closure order, InetAddress methods, and multi-threading for multiple clients.
In summary, client-server programming uses sockets for network communication. TCP provides reliability while UDP provides speed. Practice the code patterns!
g.text: • TCP: Reliable, ordered (Socket/ServerSocket) | UDP: Fast, unreliable (DatagramSocket)
• Server: Create → Accept → Streams → Communicate → Close
• Multi-clients need threading | InetAddress for IP handling | Practice exam patterns!
